02 Fiber & Reconciliation — What Happens Between setState and Pixels

← Back to lessons

Forget "virtual DOM." That's a marketing simplification. What React actually maintains is a fiber tree — a linked data structure that represents your component hierarchy and enables interruptible rendering. This lesson shows you the real architecture.

The "Virtual DOM" Myth

If someone asks you "how does React work?" and you answer "it uses a virtual DOM to diff changes," you're giving the 2015 answer. The reality in modern React is more nuanced and more interesting.

The term "virtual DOM" describes a concept: keep a JavaScript representation of the DOM, diff it against the previous version, and apply only the changes. This is true at a high level. But the implementation — React Fiber — is far more sophisticated than "a copy of the DOM in memory."

Dan Abramov has said the "virtual DOM" framing is misleading. React doesn't create a lightweight copy of the DOM. It creates a fiber tree — a linked list of fiber nodes that represent components, not DOM elements. The DOM diffing is a consequence, not the core idea. The core idea is scheduling work.

What Is a Fiber?

A fiber is a JavaScript object that represents a unit of work. There's one fiber for every component instance and every DOM element in your tree. Think of it like a node in a bureaucratic filing system — it tracks everything React needs to know about that piece of your UI.

// Simplified fiber node structure
{
  tag: FunctionComponent,    // What type of thing is this?
  type: ArticleCard,         // The component function itself
  key: 'article-42',         // For reconciliation identity

  stateNode: null,           // For class components: the instance
                             // For host elements: the DOM node

  memoizedProps: { ... },    // Props from the last render
  memoizedState: { ... },    // State from the last render (hooks)
  pendingProps: { ... },     // Props for the current render

  // Tree structure (not parent/children like DOM)
  return: parentFiber,       // Points to parent
  child: firstChildFiber,    // Points to first child
  sibling: nextSiblingFiber, // Points to next sibling

  // For hooks
  hooks: linkedListOfHooks,  // useState, useEffect, etc.

  // Effect tracking
  flags: Update | Placement, // What needs to happen to this node
  alternate: previousFiber,  // The "other" tree (double-buffering)
}

The key insight: Fibers use a linked list structure (return/child/sibling pointers) instead of a nested tree (parent/children arrays). Why? Because a linked list can be traversed incrementally. React can process one fiber, then pause and yield to the browser for paint or input handling, then pick up exactly where it left off. A recursive tree traversal can't be paused mid-way — a linked list can.

Double Buffering: Current and Work-in-Progress

React maintains two fiber trees at all times:

  • Current tree: Represents what's currently on screen. The "source of truth" for the rendered UI.
  • Work-in-progress (WIP) tree: Being built during the current render. Contains the new state, new props, new children.

When a render completes, React swaps them. The WIP tree becomes the current tree. The old current tree becomes available for reuse in the next render (React recycles fiber nodes to reduce garbage collection pressure).

This is exactly like double-buffering in video games. You render the next frame to a back buffer while displaying the front buffer. When the frame is complete, you swap the pointers. The user never sees a half-rendered frame. React uses the same technique so you never see a half-committed DOM update.

// Conceptually, the swap looks like this:

// During render:
currentTree   -> [what's on screen]
wipTree       -> [being built with new state]

// After commit:
currentTree   -> [the new tree, now on screen]
wipTree       -> [the old tree, recycled for next render]

// Each fiber's .alternate points to its counterpart
currentFiber.alternate === wipFiber
wipFiber.alternate === currentFiber

The Reconciliation Algorithm

Reconciliation is the process of determining what changed between the current tree and the new element tree (from your component functions' return values). Here's how it works:

Rule 1: Different types = destroy and rebuild

If a fiber's type changes (say from <div> to <span>, or from <ArticleCard> to <VideoCard>), React tears down the entire subtree and rebuilds from scratch. All state in that subtree is lost.

// Changing the root element type destroys state:

// Before:
<div>
  <Counter />   // Has state: count = 5
</div>

// After:
<section>
  <Counter />   // State RESET to 0 — new instance
</section>

Rule 2: Same type = update in place

If the type is the same, React keeps the existing DOM node/component instance and updates only the changed attributes or props.

// Same type, different props — updates in place:

// Before:
<button className="primary" disabled={false}>Submit</button>

// After:
<button className="primary" disabled={true}>Submit</button>

// React only calls: button.disabled = true
// The DOM node itself is preserved

Rule 3: Keys identify children in lists

This is where keys earn their salary. Without keys, React matches children by index. With keys, React matches children by identity.

Without keys (index matching)
// Before:
<ul>
  <li>Apple</li>    // index 0
  <li>Banana</li>   // index 1
</ul>

// After (prepend Cherry):
<ul>
  <li>Cherry</li>   // index 0 → mutate "Apple" to "Cherry"
  <li>Apple</li>    // index 1 → mutate "Banana" to "Apple"
  <li>Banana</li>   // index 2 → create new
</ul>
// 3 mutations!
With keys (identity matching)
// Before:
<ul>
  <li key="a">Apple</li>
  <li key="b">Banana</li>
</ul>

// After (prepend Cherry):
<ul>
  <li key="c">Cherry</li>  // new key → insert
  <li key="a">Apple</li>   // same key → keep
  <li key="b">Banana</li>  // same key → keep
</ul>
// 1 insertion!

Keys aren't just about performance. They're about correctness. If a list item has local state (like an input field), index-based matching will put the wrong state on the wrong item when the list order changes. Using the array index as key is only safe when the list never reorders, never has items inserted/removed from the middle, and items have no local state.

Never use key={Math.random()}. This gives every element a new key every render, which means React destroys and recreates every component in the list on every render. It defeats the entire purpose of reconciliation.

Concurrent Rendering: The Fiber Payoff

The entire Fiber rewrite (React 16+) was done for one reason: to make rendering interruptible. The old "stack reconciler" (pre-Fiber) processed the component tree recursively in one synchronous pass. If your tree was large, the browser's main thread was blocked for the entire duration — no animations, no input handling, nothing.

Fiber's linked list structure allows React to:

  1. Break work into chunks: Process one fiber, then check if there's higher-priority work (like user input)
  2. Pause and resume: Stop mid-tree and continue later, because each fiber node has pointers to its next piece of work
  3. Abort and restart: If new state arrives while rendering, discard the WIP tree and start fresh with newer data
  4. Assign priorities: User interactions get high priority, data fetches get lower priority
// Simplified work loop (what React does internally)
function workLoop(deadline) {
  while (nextUnitOfWork && deadline.timeRemaining() > 1) {
    nextUnitOfWork = performUnitOfWork(nextUnitOfWork);
  }

  if (!nextUnitOfWork && wipRoot) {
    commitRoot(); // All work done — commit to DOM
  }

  requestIdleCallback(workLoop); // Schedule next chunk
}

function performUnitOfWork(fiber) {
  // 1. Call the component function, get children
  // 2. Create fibers for children (reconcile)
  // 3. Return the next fiber to work on
  if (fiber.child) return fiber.child;
  let nextFiber = fiber;
  while (nextFiber) {
    if (nextFiber.sibling) return nextFiber.sibling;
    nextFiber = nextFiber.return; // Walk up to parent
  }
}

The traversal order matters. React walks the fiber tree depth-first: process a node, go to its first child, go to its sibling, walk back up to the parent. This means the return/child/sibling pointers encode the entire traversal — no call stack needed. That's why it's interruptible: the "where am I?" state is in the data structure, not on the JavaScript call stack.

Concurrent features you use today

Fiber enables several features you're already using:

  • useTransition: Mark a state update as non-urgent. React renders it in the background without blocking user input.
  • useDeferredValue: Show stale data while the new data is being rendered. Like a debounce, but React-aware.
  • Suspense: Pause rendering at a boundary while waiting for async data, show a fallback, then seamlessly swap in the content.
  • Streaming SSR: Send HTML in chunks as each Suspense boundary resolves, instead of waiting for the entire page.
// useTransition in practice: search with a non-blocking filter
function ArticleSearch({ articles }: Props) {
  const [query, setQuery] = useState('');
  const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition();

  function handleChange(e: ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>) {
    // The input update is urgent (show what user typed immediately)
    setQuery(e.target.value);

    // The filter is non-urgent (can render in background)
    startTransition(() => {
      setFilteredArticles(
        articles.filter(a => a.title.includes(e.target.value))
      );
    });
  }

  return (
    <div>
      <input value={query} onChange={handleChange} />
      {isPending && <Spinner />}
      <ArticleList articles={filteredArticles} />
    </div>
  );
}

The Commit Phase: When the DOM Actually Changes

After reconciliation completes, React has a list of "effects" — DOM mutations that need to happen. The commit phase applies these synchronously (it cannot be interrupted — you don't want a half-updated DOM).

The commit phase has three sub-phases:

  1. Before mutation: Read from the DOM (like getSnapshotBeforeUpdate in class components)
  2. Mutation: Apply all DOM insertions, updates, and deletions
  3. Layout: Run useLayoutEffect callbacks (sync, before paint). Run useEffect callbacks (async, after paint).

The commit phase is always synchronous. No matter how fancy the concurrent render phase is, the commit phase runs in one uninterrupted pass. This guarantees the user never sees an inconsistent DOM. The render phase is where React does the smart scheduling; the commit phase is the reliable, boring part that just applies changes.

Check Your Understanding

1. What is a fiber, in concrete terms?

A fiber is a JavaScript object (a plain POJO) that represents one unit of work in the React tree. It has pointers to its parent (return), first child (child), and next sibling (sibling), plus it stores the component's type, props, state, hooks, and effect flags.

2. Why does React use a linked list structure instead of a recursive tree?

Because a linked list can be traversed incrementally. React can process one node, check if the browser needs to handle user input, and resume later. A recursive traversal stores its progress on the call stack, which can't be paused.

3. What happens when you change a component's root element type (e.g., div to section)?

React tears down the entire subtree (unmounts all components, destroys all DOM nodes, loses all state) and rebuilds from scratch. Different type = new identity = full reset.

4. Explain why key={index} is dangerous for lists that can reorder.

React uses keys to match old and new children. If you use indices and insert an item at position 0, every item's index shifts. React thinks item at index 0 changed, index 1 changed, etc., and updates every DOM node. Worse: if items have local state (inputs, focus), the state stays at the old index position, getting attached to the wrong item.

5. Can the commit phase be interrupted?

No. The commit phase is always synchronous. It applies all DOM mutations in one pass to prevent the user from seeing an inconsistent UI. Only the render (reconciliation) phase can be interrupted by concurrent features.

Exercise: Break Reconciliation on Purpose

  1. Create a list of items with input fields. Use key={index}. Add items to the beginning of the list. Watch the inputs get scrambled. Then fix it with proper keys.
  2. Wrap a component in a div, then change it to a span. Observe that all child state resets. Use React DevTools to verify the component unmounts and remounts.
  3. Use React DevTools Profiler: trigger a re-render and look at the flame chart. Identify which components re-rendered and how long each took.
